questions.stem: The assignment model is a special case of the transportation model in which:
options: The supply at each source and the demand at each destination are each one unit
Supply is always greater than demand
questions.explanation: In an assignment problem each source supplies exactly one unit and each destination demands exactly one unit, so one agent is matched to one task. This one-to-one structure is what distinguishes it from a general transportation problem.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the linear programming formulation of a balanced assignment problem, every supply value and every demand value equals:
questions.explanation: Each row sum (supply) and each column sum (demand) is set to $1$, for example $x_{AR} + x_{AA} + x_{AD} + x_{AC} = 1$. This forces exactly one assignment per agent and per task.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The standard method used in the slides to solve the assignment problem is the:
questions.explanation: The assignment problem is solved by the Hungarian method, which builds an opportunity-cost table through row and column reductions and then makes assignments at the zero cells. The stepping-stone method is for transportation problems.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The first step of the Hungarian method is to:
options: Subtract the minimum value in each row from all entries of that row
questions.explanation: Row reduction comes first: subtract the smallest entry in each row from every entry in that row, creating at least one zero per row. Column reduction follows, producing the opportunity-cost table.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: After row reduction, the Hungarian method performs:
options: Column reduction, subtracting the minimum of each column from all its entries
questions.explanation: Following row reduction, each column has its minimum subtracted from all its entries (column reduction). The result is the completed opportunity-cost table in which assignments are sought at the zeros.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the Hungarian method, assignments are made to cells that contain:
questions.explanation: Once the opportunity-cost table is built, unique assignments are made only at cells containing zeros, with at most one assignment per row and per column. Zeros represent positions of least opportunity cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: An optimal assignment is reached when the number of independent unique (zero) assignments equals:
questions.explanation: Optimality occurs when the number of independent unique assignments equals the number of rows, which equals the number of columns in a balanced problem. Then every agent is matched to a distinct task at zero opportunity cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: If fewer unique assignments can be made than there are rows, the Hungarian method applies a:
options: Line test, covering all zeros with the minimum number of lines
questions.explanation: When a full set of assignments is not yet possible, all zeros are crossed out with the fewest horizontal and vertical lines. If fewer than $m$ lines suffice, the table is revised; when $m$ lines are needed, $m$ assignments exist (optimum).
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the line-test revision step, after covering zeros with fewer than $m$ lines, you:
options: Subtract the minimum uncovered value from all uncovered values and add it to cells where two lines cross
questions.explanation: Subtract the smallest uncovered entry from every uncovered entry, add it to each entry at the intersection of two covering lines, and leave singly-covered entries unchanged. Then repeat the line test on the new table.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The ACC basketball example assigns four teams of officials to four games to minimize total distance. The optimal total distance is:
questions.explanation: The Hungarian method gives an optimal assignment totalling $90 + 100 + 140 + 120 = 450$ miles (for example A to Atlanta, B to Raleigh, C to Dallas, D to Clemson). An alternative optimal assignment also totals $450$ miles.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the ACC example, one optimal assignment sends official A to Atlanta ($90$), B to Raleigh ($100$), C to Dallas ($140$), D to Clemson ($120$). The total is:
questions.explanation: Summing the assigned distances gives $90 + 100 + 140 + 120 = 450$ miles. This matches the optimal total found by the Hungarian method.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: When supply exceeds demand in an assignment problem (more agents than tasks), the tableau is balanced by adding a:
questions.explanation: If there are more agents (supply) than tasks (demand), a dummy column with zero costs is added so the tableau becomes square. A dummy row is added instead when demand exceeds supply.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: When demand exceeds supply in an assignment problem, the tableau is balanced by adding a:
questions.explanation: More tasks than agents means demand exceeds supply, so a dummy row (a fictitious agent) with zero costs is added to square the matrix. This lets the Hungarian method proceed on a balanced problem.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: A prohibited assignment (one that cannot be allowed) is handled in the Hungarian method by giving that cell:
options: A very large relative cost $M$ so it is never selected
questions.explanation: A prohibited assignment is assigned a prohibitively large cost $M$, ensuring the method never chooses it in the optimal solution. This mirrors how prohibited routes are handled in transportation problems.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: Once an assignment is made to a particular row and column in the Hungarian method:
options: More assignments may be made in that same row or column
All zeros in the table are removed
No further assignment can be made in that row or column
questions.explanation: Because each agent does one task and each task uses one agent, once a cell is selected no other assignment may be made in its row or its column. This enforces the one-to-one matching.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The presence of multiple zeros allowing different complete assignments indicates that the assignment problem has:
questions.explanation: When several zero patterns each give a full set of assignments, there are multiple optimal solutions. The ACC example, for instance, has two distinct assignments both totalling $450$ miles.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: To solve a maximization assignment problem (for example maximizing ratings) by the Hungarian method, a common first step is to:
options: Convert it to a minimization by subtracting every entry from the largest entry (an opportunity-loss matrix)
questions.explanation: The Hungarian method minimizes, so a maximization problem is first converted to an equivalent minimization, typically by subtracting each value from the maximum value to form a loss matrix. The method is then applied as usual.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the assignment LP, the constraint $x_{AR} + x_{BR} + x_{CR} + x_{DR} = 1$ expresses that:
options: Game (site) R is officiated by exactly one team
Exactly four teams cover game R
questions.explanation: This column constraint forces the total assignment to site R across all four officials to equal one, meaning game R is handled by exactly one team of officials. Row constraints similarly ensure each official takes exactly one game.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: How does the assignment model differ from a general transportation model?
options: It maximizes instead of always minimizing
Each supply and each demand is limited to one unit, giving a one-to-one matching
questions.explanation: The assignment model is a transportation model specialized so that every supply and demand equals one, producing a one-to-one assignment of agents to tasks. Otherwise its structure and objective mirror the transportation problem.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: A balanced assignment problem with $m$ agents and $m$ tasks is optimal when the minimum number of lines needed to cover all zeros equals:
questions.explanation: When exactly $m$ covering lines are required to cross out all zeros, $m$ independent assignments can be made and the solution is optimal. Fewer than $m$ lines means the table must be revised and the test repeated.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: An entry of the opportunity-cost table created by the Hungarian method represents the:
options: Relative penalty (opportunity cost) of not using the best option in that row and column
questions.explanation: After row and column reductions, each entry is an opportunity cost: how much more than the cheapest available option that assignment would cost. Zeros mark the least-cost choices around which assignments are built.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The ACC example assigns four teams of officials to four games to minimize total travel distance.
questions.solution: One optimal assignment sends A to Atlanta ($90$), B to Raleigh ($100$), C to Dallas ($140$), and D to Clemson ($120$). Summing these distances gives a total of {{1}} miles. Because a second complete assignment achieves the same total, the problem has {{2}} optimal solutions.
questions.explanation: The distances sum to $90 + 100 + 140 + 120 = 450$ miles; a second assignment also totals $450$, so there are multiple optimal solutions.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The Hungarian method builds an opportunity-cost table in two reduction steps.
questions.solution: First, subtract the minimum entry of each {{1}} from all its entries. Second, subtract the minimum entry of each {{2}} from all its entries. Assignments are then made at cells containing a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Row reduction then column reduction produce the opportunity-cost table; assignments are made at the zeros.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: An assignment problem is balanced before the Hungarian method is applied.
questions.solution: If there are more agents than tasks (supply exceeds demand), a dummy {{1}} is added. If there are more tasks than agents (demand exceeds supply), a dummy {{2}} is added. Entries in any dummy line are given a cost of {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Surplus agents need a dummy column; surplus tasks need a dummy row; dummy costs are $0$.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The line test decides optimality in the Hungarian method.
questions.solution: Cover all zeros using the {{1}} number of horizontal and vertical lines. If the number of lines equals the number of rows $m$, then $m$ unique assignments exist and the solution is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Cover zeros with the fewest lines; when that count equals $m$, an optimal set of $m$ assignments is available.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: When fewer than $m$ lines cover all zeros, the opportunity-cost table is revised.
questions.solution: Let $d$ be the smallest uncovered entry. Subtract $d$ from every {{1}} entry and add $d$ to every entry lying at the {{2}} of two covering lines. Singly-covered entries are left unchanged, and the line test is then repeated.
questions.explanation: Subtract the minimum uncovered value from all uncovered entries and add it at the intersections of covering lines; repeat the line test.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: A prohibited assignment must be excluded from the optimal solution.
questions.solution: Such a cell is assigned a very {{1}} relative cost, denoted $M$, so it is never selected. This is the same device used for a prohibited route in a {{2}} problem.
questions.explanation: A prohibited assignment gets a large cost $M$, mirroring the prohibited-route device of the transportation problem.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research
